name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard (MASVS) 2.0
description: The OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard (MASVS) is a comprehensive security standard for mobile app security. It defines two security verification levels (MAS-L1 and MAS-L2) and covers all aspects of mobile app security including data storage, cryptography, authentication, network communication, platform interaction, code quality, resilience against reverse engineering, and privacy. MASVS 2.0 provides a baseline for mobile app security that can be used during design, development, and testing of iOS and Android mobile apps.

controls.title: Secure Storage of Sensitive Data
controls.description: The app securely stores sensitive data. Apps handle sensitive data coming from many sources such as the user, the backend, system services or other apps on the device and usually need to store it locally. The storage locations may be private to the app (e.g. its internal storage) or be public and therefore accessible by the user or other installed apps (e.g. public folders such as Downloads). This control ensures that any sensitive data that is intentionally stored by the app is properly protected independently of the target location.
controls.discussion: MASVS-STORAGE: Sensitive data must be stored securely regardless of the storage location. Developers should use platform-provided secure storage mechanisms such as the Android Keystore or iOS Keychain for credentials and cryptographic keys. Application-specific data should be stored in the app's private storage directory with appropriate file permissions. Sensitive data should be encrypted at rest using strong, current cryptographic algorithms.

controls.title: Prevention of Sensitive Data Leakage
controls.description: The app prevents leakage of sensitive data. There are cases when sensitive data is unintentionally stored or exposed to publicly accessible locations; typically as a side-effect of using certain APIs, system capabilities such as backups or logs. This control covers this kind of unintentional leaks where the developer actually has a way to prevent it.
controls.discussion: MASVS-STORAGE: Developers must be aware of platform features that may unintentionally expose sensitive data, including system logs, clipboard, app backups, keyboard caches, and screenshot captures. Sensitive data should be excluded from backups, logging should be reviewed to ensure no sensitive data is written, and text fields containing sensitive input should disable autocomplete and keyboard learning features.

controls.title: Strong Cryptography
controls.description: The app employs current strong cryptography and uses it according to industry best practices. Cryptography plays an especially important role in securing the user's data — even more so in a mobile environment, where attackers having physical access to the user's device is a likely scenario. This control covers general cryptography best practices, which are typically defined in external standards.
controls.discussion: MASVS-CRYPTO: Applications should use only well-established cryptographic algorithms and implementations provided by the platform (e.g. Android JCA, iOS CryptoKit). Avoid deprecated or weak algorithms such as MD5, SHA1, DES, or RC4. Use AES-256 for symmetric encryption, RSA-2048 or ECDSA for asymmetric operations, and SHA-256 or stronger for hashing. Ensure proper use of initialization vectors, padding schemes, and modes of operation.

controls.title: Cryptographic Key Management
controls.description: The app performs key management according to industry best practices. Even the strongest cryptography would be compromised by poor key management. This control covers the management of cryptographic keys throughout their lifecycle, including key generation, storage and protection.
controls.discussion: MASVS-CRYPTO: Cryptographic keys must be generated using secure random number generators and stored in platform-provided secure storage (Android Keystore, iOS Keychain). Keys should never be hardcoded in the application source code, stored in shared preferences or plain-text files, or derived from predictable values. Key rotation policies should be implemented, and keys should be invalidated when no longer needed.

controls.title: Secure Authentication and Authorization Protocols
controls.description: The app uses secure authentication and authorization protocols and follows the relevant best practices. Most apps connecting to a remote endpoint require user authentication and also enforce some kind of authorization. While the enforcement of these mechanisms must be on the remote endpoint, the apps also have to ensure that they follow all the relevant best practices to ensure a secure use of the involved protocols.
controls.discussion: MASVS-AUTH: Apps should use well-established authentication protocols such as OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect. Session tokens should be sufficiently random and transmitted securely. All authentication decisions must be enforced server-side, not solely on the client. Sensitive operations should require step-up authentication, and session management should include proper timeout and invalidation mechanisms.

controls.title: Secure Local Authentication
controls.description: The app performs local authentication securely according to the platform best practices. Many apps allow users to authenticate via biometrics or a local PIN code. These authentication mechanisms need to be correctly implemented. Additionally, some apps might not have a remote endpoint, and rely fully on local app authentication.
controls.discussion: MASVS-AUTH: Local authentication using biometrics or PIN must leverage platform-provided APIs (BiometricPrompt on Android, LAContext on iOS) and should be backed by hardware-secured cryptographic keys. Biometric authentication should use the strongest available class and should not fall back to weaker methods silently. Authentication results should be cryptographically tied to a key operation rather than relying on a simple boolean check.

controls.title: Additional Authentication for Sensitive Operations
controls.description: The app secures sensitive operations with additional authentication. Some additional form of authentication is often desirable for sensitive actions inside the app. This can be done in different ways (biometric, pin, MFA code generator, email, deep links, etc) and they all need to be implemented securely.
controls.discussion: MASVS-AUTH: Sensitive operations such as financial transactions, profile changes, or data exports should require step-up authentication. The additional authentication factor should be verified server-side when a remote endpoint is involved. Time-limited tokens or one-time codes should be used, and the authentication challenge should be contextually appropriate to the sensitivity of the operation being performed.

controls.title: Secure Network Traffic
controls.description: The app secures all network traffic according to the current best practices. Ensuring data privacy and integrity of any data in transit is critical for any app that communicates over the network. This is typically done by encrypting data and authenticating the remote endpoint, as TLS does. However, there are many ways for a developer to disable the platform secure defaults, or bypass them completely by using low-level APIs or third-party libraries. This control ensures that the app is in fact setting up secure connections in any situation.
controls.discussion: MASVS-NETWORK: All network communication must use TLS 1.2 or higher with strong cipher suites. The app should not override default platform TLS validation, accept self-signed certificates, or disable certificate validation. Network Security Configuration (Android) or App Transport Security (iOS) should be properly configured to enforce secure defaults. Clear-text HTTP traffic should be disabled entirely.

controls.title: Identity Pinning
controls.description: The app performs identity pinning for all remote endpoints under the developer's control. Instead of trusting all the default root CAs of the framework or device, this control will make sure that only very specific CAs are trusted. This practice is typically called certificate pinning or public key pinning.
controls.discussion: MASVS-NETWORK: Certificate or public key pinning should be implemented for all connections to endpoints under the developer's control. Pin against the public key or a hash of the public key rather than the full certificate to allow for certificate rotation. Implement backup pins and a recovery strategy in case pins need to be updated. Pinning validation failures should be logged and reported, and the app should fail securely by refusing the connection.

controls.title: Secure IPC Mechanisms
controls.description: The app uses IPC mechanisms securely. Apps typically use platform provided IPC mechanisms to intentionally expose data or functionality. Both installed apps and the user are able to interact with the app in many different ways. This control ensures that all interactions involving IPC mechanisms happen securely.
controls.discussion: MASVS-PLATFORM: Inter-process communication mechanisms such as Intents (Android), URL Schemes, Universal Links (iOS), and Content Providers must be secured with proper access controls. Exported components should be minimized, and those that must be exported should validate all incoming data. Sensitive data should not be sent via IPC mechanisms that can be intercepted by other apps. Deep links should be validated to prevent unauthorized access to app functionality.

controls.title: Secure WebView Configuration
controls.description: The app uses WebViews securely. WebViews are typically used by apps that have a need for increased control over the UI. This control ensures that WebViews are configured securely to prevent sensitive data leakage as well as sensitive functionality exposure (e.g. via JavaScript bridges to native code).
controls.discussion: MASVS-PLATFORM: WebViews must be configured with security in mind. JavaScript should be disabled unless explicitly required. JavaScript-to-native bridges should be minimized and only expose necessary functionality with proper input validation. WebViews should only load content from trusted sources and enforce secure content loading policies. File access from WebViews should be disabled, and the WebView cache and storage should be cleared when handling sensitive data.

controls.title: Secure User Interface
controls.description: The app uses the user interface securely. Sensitive data has to be displayed in the UI in many situations (e.g. passwords, credit card details, OTP codes in notifications). This control ensures that this data doesn't end up being unintentionally leaked due to platform mechanisms such as auto-generated screenshots or accidentally disclosed via shoulder surfing or sharing the device with another person.
controls.discussion: MASVS-PLATFORM: Sensitive data displayed in the UI should be masked or hidden where appropriate. The app should prevent screenshots and screen recording on screens displaying sensitive information by using platform-provided flags (FLAG_SECURE on Android, appropriate UITextField settings on iOS). Notifications should not contain sensitive data in their preview text. Sensitive input fields should use secure text entry and prevent content from appearing in the app switcher thumbnail.

controls.title: Up-to-Date Platform Version
controls.description: The app requires an up-to-date platform version. Every release of the mobile OS includes security patches and new security features. By supporting older versions, apps stay vulnerable to well-known threats. This control ensures that the app is running on an up-to-date platform version so that users have the latest security protections.
controls.discussion: MASVS-CODE: Apps should set a minimum supported OS version that receives active security updates. The minimum SDK/API level should be reviewed and updated regularly to drop support for OS versions that are no longer receiving security patches. Users running outdated OS versions should be informed of the security risks and encouraged to update their devices.

controls.title: App Update Enforcement
controls.description: The app has a mechanism for enforcing app updates. Sometimes critical vulnerabilities are discovered in the app when it is already in production. This control ensures that there is a mechanism to force the users to update the app before they can continue using it.
controls.discussion: MASVS-CODE: Implement a forced update mechanism that can require users to update to the latest version when critical security vulnerabilities are discovered. Use platform-provided in-app update APIs (Google Play In-App Updates, App Store update mechanisms) where available. The update check should be performed server-side to allow dynamic control over which versions are allowed, and the app should gracefully handle the update flow.

controls.title: Secure Software Components
controls.description: The app only uses software components without known vulnerabilities. To be truly secure, a full whitebox assessment should have been performed on all app components. However, as it usually happens with third-party components this is not always feasible. This control covers low-hanging fruit cases, such as those that can be detected just by scanning libraries for known vulnerabilities.
controls.discussion: MASVS-CODE: Maintain a complete inventory of all third-party libraries and their versions (software bill of materials). Regularly scan dependencies for known vulnerabilities using automated tools and update vulnerable components promptly. Only use libraries from trusted sources, and evaluate the security posture and maintenance status of third-party dependencies before incorporating them.

controls.title: Input Validation and Sanitization
controls.description: The app validates and sanitizes all untrusted inputs. Apps have many data entry points including the UI, IPC, the network, the file system, etc. This incoming data might have been inadvertently modified by untrusted actors and may lead to bypass of critical security checks as well as classical injection attacks such as SQL injection, XSS or insecure deserialization.
controls.discussion: MASVS-CODE: All input from external sources must be treated as untrusted and validated before use. Implement input validation using allowlists where possible, and sanitize data appropriately based on its intended use context. Protect against injection attacks by using parameterized queries, safe APIs, and proper output encoding. Deserialize data using safe methods and validate the structure and content of deserialized objects.

controls.title: Platform Integrity Validation
controls.description: The app validates the integrity of the platform. Running on a platform that has been tampered with can be very dangerous for apps, as this may disable certain security features, putting the data of the app at risk. Trusting the platform is essential for many of the MASVS controls relying on the platform being secure (e.g. secure storage, biometrics, sandboxing, etc.).
controls.discussion: MASVS-RESILIENCE: Apps handling sensitive data should detect and respond to rooted (Android) or jailbroken (iOS) devices. Use platform-provided APIs and multiple detection techniques to identify compromised environments. The app's response should be proportional to the risk — options include warning the user, restricting functionality, or refusing to run. Detection logic should be resilient to common bypass techniques.

controls.title: Anti-Tampering Mechanisms
controls.description: The app implements anti-tampering mechanisms. Apps run on a user-controlled device, and without proper protections it's relatively easy to run a modified version locally (e.g. to cheat in a game, or enable premium features without paying), or upload a backdoored version of it to third-party app stores. This control tries to ensure the integrity of the app's intended functionality by preventing modifications to the original code and resources.
controls.discussion: MASVS-RESILIENCE: Implement integrity checks to detect modifications to the app's code and resources at runtime. Use code signing verification, checksum validation of critical files, and tamper detection libraries. The app should respond appropriately when tampering is detected, such as refusing to run or alerting the server. Multiple layered detection mechanisms should be used to increase resilience against bypass attempts.

controls.title: Anti-Static Analysis Mechanisms
controls.description: The app implements anti-static analysis mechanisms. Understanding the internals of an app is typically the first step towards tampering with it (either dynamically, or statically). This control tries to impede comprehension by making it as difficult as possible to figure out how an app works using static analysis.
controls.discussion: MASVS-RESILIENCE: Apply code obfuscation techniques to make reverse engineering more difficult, including identifier renaming, string encryption, control flow obfuscation, and dead code insertion. Use platform-provided tools (ProGuard/R8 for Android) and commercial obfuscation solutions where appropriate. Obfuscation should be applied consistently across the entire codebase, with particular attention to security-sensitive code paths.

controls.title: Anti-Dynamic Analysis Techniques
controls.description: The app implements anti-dynamic analysis techniques. Sometimes pure static analysis is very difficult and time consuming so it typically goes hand in hand with dynamic analysis. Observing and manipulating an app during runtime makes it much easier to decipher its behavior. This control aims to make it as difficult as possible to perform dynamic analysis, as well as prevent dynamic instrumentation which could allow an attacker to modify the code at runtime.
controls.discussion: MASVS-RESILIENCE: Implement runtime integrity checks to detect debugging, instrumentation frameworks (Frida, Xposed), and hooking attempts. Use multiple detection techniques including debugger detection, process monitoring, and integrity verification of critical functions. The app should respond to detected dynamic analysis attempts by modifying behavior, terminating, or alerting the server. Detection mechanisms should be distributed throughout the code to avoid single points of bypass.

controls.title: Data Minimization
controls.description: The app minimizes access to sensitive data and resources. Apps should only request access to the data they absolutely need for their functionality and always with informed consent from the user. This control ensures that apps practice data minimization and restrict access control, reducing the potential impact of data breaches or leaks. Furthermore, apps should share data with third parties only when necessary, and this should include enforcing that third-party SDKs operate based on user consent.
controls.discussion: MASVS-PRIVACY: Request only the minimum permissions necessary for the app's core functionality and explain to users why each permission is needed. Implement runtime permission requests at the point of use rather than requesting all permissions at install time. Regularly audit third-party SDKs for data collection practices and ensure they respect user consent signals. Maintain awareness of the SDK supply chain and ensure no data is unnecessarily passed down the chain of dependencies.

controls.title: User Identification Prevention
controls.description: The app prevents identification of the user. Protecting user identity is crucial. This control emphasizes the use of unlinkability techniques like data abstraction, anonymization and pseudonymization to prevent user identification and tracking. Another key aspect is to establish technical barriers when employing complex fingerprint-like signals (e.g. device IDs, IP addresses, behavioral patterns) for specific purposes.
controls.discussion: MASVS-PRIVACY: Implement data anonymization and pseudonymization techniques to protect user identity. Avoid using persistent device identifiers for tracking purposes, and use purpose-specific identifiers that cannot be correlated across different contexts. Ensure that fingerprinting signals used for legitimate purposes (e.g. fraud detection) are isolated and cannot be repurposed for user tracking. Comply with platform-specific privacy requirements such as App Tracking Transparency on iOS.

controls.title: Transparency in Data Collection
controls.description: The app is transparent about data collection and usage. Users have the right to know how their data is being used. This control ensures that apps provide clear information about data collection, storage, and sharing practices, including any behavior a user wouldn't reasonably expect, such as background data collection. Apps should also adhere to platform guidelines on data declarations.
controls.discussion: MASVS-PRIVACY: Provide clear, accessible privacy notices that explain what data is collected, how it is used, and with whom it is shared. Ensure that privacy labels and data safety declarations submitted to app stores accurately reflect the app's actual data practices. Disclose any background data collection or unexpected data processing activities, and provide users with meaningful notice before engaging in such activities.

controls.title: User Data Control
controls.description: The app offers user control over their data. Users should have control over their data. This control ensures that apps provide mechanisms for users to manage, delete, and modify their data, and change privacy settings as needed (e.g. to revoke consent). Additionally, apps should re-prompt for consent and update their transparency disclosures when they require more data than initially specified.
controls.discussion: MASVS-PRIVACY: Implement user-facing controls for managing personal data, including the ability to view, export, correct, and delete their data. Provide granular privacy settings that allow users to control consent for specific data processing activities. When the app needs to collect additional data beyond what was originally consented to, re-prompt users for explicit consent and update privacy disclosures accordingly. Support account deletion requests in compliance with platform requirements and applicable regulations.
